Web Analytics Events
•
Web Analytics Pages
•
Web Analytics Tags
•
Generic Data Stream Type
Ads
In the world of digital marketing, the ads data stream type is where you can find the data for your display
ads campaigns. This data stream type contains the delivery data that comes from these campaigns. For
example, how many times an ad has been seen, clicked or how much money it cost. The ads data stream
type can be used with providers, such as Facebook Ads, Google Ads, Twitter Ads, Bing Ads and more.
Ads Verification Blocking
The ads verification blocking data stream type tracks data where ads were blocked by ad verification
platforms, and therefore weren’t served. This usually pertains to ads that were blocked due to a violation
of the platform’s terms and conditions and either disrupted user experience or violated brand safety. You
can use this data stream type with providers, such as IAS, and Double Verify.
Buy Data
The ‘buy data’ data stream type is intended for ingesting data relating to planned advertising activity,
sometimes referred to as ‘planned data’ or a ‘media plan’. Used in Media Transparency Center
implementations, it facilitates the bulk creation of Insertion Orders (or IOs for short), which themselves
represent the scope and details of the said activity, with information such as the IO start and end dates,
IO cost type, IO rate and IO budget. It’s also used to create an association between IOs and their related
delivery data, thereby unifying planned with actuals. This data stream type can be used to ingest media
plans from systems, such as Salesforce, Mediaocean, Nexelus, and Netsuite.
83

=== PAGE 88 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Buy Data Conversions
Buy data (ingested using the ‘buy data’ data stream type) can contain IOs that are associated with
conversion tags. However, not all conversion tags associated with a certain delivery item under an IO, are
necessarily relevant for that IO’s cost calculations. The buy data conversions data stream type allows you
to associate between existing IOs and the conversion tags of relevance. In a sense, this data stream type
can be thought of as a ‘conversion tag filter’ for IOs, and is only used as an add-on to an existing use of
the ‘buy data’ data stream type.
CRM Leads
The CRM leads data stream type is intended for ingesting data relating to the potential prospects of a
business, commonly known as ‘Leads’. Designed to keep a historical record of each lead’s evolution over
time, this data stream type is equipped with slowly changing type 2 dimensions, such as lead stage, lead
status and lead modified date. The CRM leads data stream type can be used to ingest data from lead
generation platforms, such as Marketing Cloud Account Engagement (Pardot), Hubspot Marketing and
Intercom, as well as from CRM platforms, such as Salesforce, Sugar CRM, and CRM Creatio.
Competitive
The competitive data stream type, which is used side by side with the social objects data stream type,
allows you to compare yourself to your competitor by displaying various social metrics side by side from
the two data stream types. The purpose of this data stream type is to allow you to view your competitors'
data separately from your own data. This data stream type is suitable for any social media platforms,
such as Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, and YouTube.
Conversion Tag
The conversion tag data stream type is intended for ingesting data related to the various 'calls to action'
tied to your campaigns, also known as conversions. You can see what particular conversion tag prompted
your audience to complete a call to action, and whether this happened after they viewed your ad or after
they clicked it. This data stream type also includes data related to the monetary aspects of your
conversions for example, how much a conversion cost or how much revenue it brought in. This data
stream type can be used with providers, such as Facebook Ads, Google Ads, Google Display&Video 360,
Twitter Ads, and more.
84

=== PAGE 89 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Conversion Tag with Keywords
Whenever an ad is displayed in a search engine result and drives a conversion, the search keywords that
led to this conversion are attributed to it. In these cases, the conversion tag with keywords data stream
type is used to ingest the conversion data along with the respective search keywords related to it. The
conversion tag with keywords data stream type is suitable for providers, such as Google Ads, Search Ads
360, and Bing Ads.
ecommerce
In the world of ecommerce, the ecommerce data stream type is where you can find different levels of
data for product catalogs, placed orders, number of purchased items within each order, and more. This
data stream type is intended for ingesting different data types, in their various forms, from ecommerce
platforms. The ecommerce data stream type can be used with providers, such as Salesforce OMS,
Amazon Seller Central, and Amazon Vendor Central.
For details on the ecommerce data stream type, see: Ecommerce Data Stream Type
Messaging
The messaging data stream type is intended for ingesting email marketing and mobile phone marketing,
as well as user journey data, which is available on some of the more advanced messaging platforms. A
message can be in the form of an email, a text message, or an app notification, and is typically
associated with measurements, such as how many people received, opened, clicked on a message, etc.
This data stream type can be used to ingest data from platforms, such as Salesforce Marketing Cloud,
Marketo, and Adobe.
Products
The products data stream type is intended for ingesting data from the commerce world. This data
generally includes entities such as products, and clients, as well as metrics, like unit purchases and cost.
The products data stream type can report on off-line purchases and ecommerce transactions, and then
tie them back to the marketing data to create a more wholesome picture of your data.
85

=== PAGE 90 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Search Keywords
The search keywords data stream type is intended for ingesting data related to ads displayed in search-
engine results whenever someone searches for the services or products using keywords offered by the
advertiser. The data is associated with campaigns and is measured by impressions, clicks, and media
cost, and other fields that make up its data set. This type of advertising has managed to acquire
numerous names, such as Search Advertising, Paid Search, Search Engine Marketing, Pay Per Click
Marketing and more. The Search Keywords data stream type is suitable for providers such as Google Ads,
Search Ads 360, and Bing Ads.
Social Element Traffic Source
The social element traffic source data stream type extends the social objects data stream type to include
traffic sources for organic social content. This allows you to identify where a user came from before
landing on a specific post or video. For example, a user can access a YouTube video via a direct link, or by
browsing a certain YouTube channel or playlist. This data stream type can be used for YouTube.
Social Listening
The social listening data stream type is used to monitor social media channels for mentions of your
brands, competitors, products, and so on. Social listening is used to track, analyze, and respond to
conversations on social media regarding your brand or product. The social listening data stream type is
suitable for platforms such as: Salesforce Social Studio, Sprinklr, and more.
Social Objects
The social objects data stream type is where you can find the organic social data that is being tracked on
various social networks and platforms. This type of data is related to the organic social activity on your
pages and posts, such as number of fans, engagement, organic reach and more. The social objects data
stream type is relevant for providers, such as Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, YouTube, Pinterest, and more.
Web Analytics
The web analytics data stream type is intended for ingesting data related to the traffic in your web pages.
This data stream type can help you analyze and assess the effectiveness of your website, based on how
many visits it gets, how much time people spend on each web page, or how many website transactions
are combined into a complete process. The web analytics data stream type can be used with providers,
such as Google Analytics, Adobe Analytics, and more.
86

=== PAGE 91 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Web Analytics Events
The web analytics event data stream type is used to analyze events, such as user interactions on a
website. An interaction can be specified according to the needs of the user. For example, a user can
decide to track the event, such as all clicks on “Add to cart”. The web analytics events data stream type is
suitable for any web analytics platform, such as Google Analytics, and Adobe Analytics.
Web Analytics Pages
The web analytics pages data stream type is used to track and monitor all data pertaining to the analytics
of your website, including page views, visits, time on page, and so on. This analysis is more granular and
allows you to get a deeper understanding and breakdown of the findings. For example you can get
analytics for specific pages and page paths. This data stream type is suitable for analytics platforms, such
as Google Analytics, and Adobe Analytics.
Web Analytics Tags
The web analytics tags data stream type is used for data that tracks goals and their completion in web
analytics. When setting a goal you can add a tag and name it, making it easier to track the goal and its
completion. This data stream type can be used for Google Analytics and more.
Generic Data Stream Type
The generic data stream type is used to ingest data that doesn’t comply with any of the existing
Marketing Cloud Intelligence data models. With all of its entities in a many-to-many relationship, dozens
of custom attributes for each entity, and a large number of custom measurements, it provides a flexibility
of structure that caters to a wide range of data types. You can use this data stream type to ingest any type
of data with various structures, such as survey responses, internal employee statistics, business process
analyses, and more.
Data Stream Types in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Marketing Cloud Intelligence includes different data stream types to map your data. A data stream
type defines which Marketing Cloud Intelligence fields are available for mapping based on your data
source. For example, if your data source contains campaign, placement, and ad ID fields, Marketing
Cloud Intelligence recognizes this as ads data and offers the Ads data stream type when mapping.
87

=== PAGE 92 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Data Stream Types in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Marketing Cloud Intelligence includes different data stream types to map your data. A data stream type
defines which Marketing Cloud Intelligence fields are available for mapping based on your data source.
For example, if your data source contains campaign, placement, and ad ID fields, Marketing Cloud
Intelligence recognizes this as ads data and offers the Ads data stream type when mapping.
Classification Data Stream Type
Classification data streams are used to apply a classification logic that exists in a secondary data source
to your primary data.
Competitive Data Stream Type
The competitive data stream type is suitable for datasets coming from public APIs and competitive
analysis tools. This data stream type is designed to fit multiple content worlds, all under the
competitive data category. It includes vendor-specific measurements like Facebook page and Twitter
likes and generic measurements suitable for multiple cases, such as lifetime likes.
CRM Data Stream Types
CRM stands for Customer Relationship Management. There are two data stream types for uploading
CRM data. One type for uploading leads data called CRM Leads, and one for opportunities data called
CRM. The CRM data stream type is equipped with slowly changing type 2 dimensions such as lead
stage, lead status, and lead modified date.
Data Stream Jobs Data Stream Type
The data stream jobs data stream type allows you to view statistics and information relevant to the
execution of your data streams. The data included in this data stream type is the same as the data
displayed in the data stream's log.
Ecommerce Data Stream Type
The ecommerce data stream type ingests different data types from ecommerce platforms. It provides
levels of data for product catalogs, placed orders, number of purchased items within each order, and
more.
Generic Data Stream Type in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The generic data stream type ingests data that doesn’t comply with any of the existing Marketing
Cloud Intelligence data models. All entities in a generic data stream type are in a many-to-many
relationship. It offers 50 custom attributes for each entity and 100 custom measurements. The generic
data stream type is flexible and caters to a wide range of data types.
Messaging Data Stream Type in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The messaging data stream type ingests email, mobile phone, and journey data. A message can be in
the form of an email, text message, or app notification. It’s typically associated with measurements
such as how many people received, opened, or clicked a message. This data stream type can be used
to ingest data from platforms such as Salesforce Marketing Cloud, Marketo, and Adobe.
Classification Data Stream Type
Classification data streams are used to apply a classification logic that exists in a secondary data source
to your primary data.
88

=== PAGE 93 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
A classification is used to group your data. Classifications can be created with calculated measurements,
and classification rules. With classification data streams you can filter and analyze your primary data
using the classification and apply classification to multiple entities at once. Read about different ways to
use the classification data stream type.
Use Case 1: Classification Data Stream Using a Parent-Child Setup
This use case looks at how to create a classification data stream using a parent-child setup.
Use Case 2: Classification Data Stream Using the VLOOKUP Function
This use case looks at how to create a classification data steam with VLOOKUP.
Use Case 1: Classification Data Stream Using a Parent-Child Setup
This use case looks at how to create a classification data stream using a parent-child setup.
RentACarWithUs is a car rental business who wants to classify its Facebook data using a classification that
only exists in their internal system. Since this particular classification isn’t part of the data that comes
from Facebook, it must be uploaded into Intelligence as a separate data stream. It can then be applied
to the Facebook data accordingly.
The finance department has a long list of PO (Purchase Order) numbers in their internal system, which
must be added to the campaign data. Create a data stream consisting of the relevant entities: Entity Key -
Campaign Key - along with the Classification Dimension - the PO Number.
To map the fields in the classification data stream, use this logic. Since the Campaign Key field in the
classification file is identical to the Facebook Ads Campaign Key, it can be mapped to the Intelligence
field by the same name. The PO Number is an attribute of Campaign Key, meaning there’s only one PO
Number for every Campaign Key. Therefore, it can be mapped as a Campaign Custom Attribute and have
its name changed as needed:
Mapping the Classification File into Intelligence
Classification File Dimensions Intelligence Fields
Campaign Key Campaign Key
Campaign Name Campaign Name
PO Number Campaign Custom Attribute
The next step is to update the PO Number values (Campaign Custom Attribute) within Facebook, with
the PO Number values from the classification data stream. This is done for the same Campaign Keys, as
shown in this diagram:
89

=== PAGE 94 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
After the data is updated, you can see that relevant PO number is assigned to each campaign within the
Facebook data stream:
Now, you must establish a parent-child relationship between the two data streams, so that they’re joined
on the basis of matching Entity Keys. This join allows the Facebook data stream to inherit attribute values
(Campaign Custom Attribute) from the classification data stream. All PO numbers are automatically
updated in the Facebook data stream for matching Campaign Keys.
In this example, set the classification data stream as the Parent and the Facebook data stream as the
Child. Any new data sources that are added, other than Facebook, automatically inherit this classification
as well.
You can also set up the Facebook data stream as the parent and classification as the child. But, this setup
is less scalable and if new data sources are added, each data source needs their own classification data
stream.
Use Case 2: Classification Data Stream Using the VLOOKUP Function
This use case looks at how to create a classification data steam with VLOOKUP.
90

=== PAGE 95 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Using a parent-child relationship isn’t always possible. Sometimes the data streams don’t have the same
Entity Key mapped, which is a prerequisite for establishing a parent-child relationship. Another way to
implement classifications is by using the VLOOKUP function. To update the PO Custom Attribute field,
map the PO Number (Campaign Custom Attribute) within the Facebook Ads data stream using this
Vlookup formula:
VLOOKUP(csv['Campaign Key'],[Campaign_Name],[PO],true,true,{'Classification Data
Stream'})
If both options are viable, using the parent-child method is more efficient and scalable than using the
Vlookup option. This is because the Vlookup option requires a dedicated Vlookup formula in the
mapping of each attribute you wish to update. Parent-child is a single configuration that automatically
updates all the attributes pertaining to a matched Entity Key. Additionally, applying classifications to new
data sources requires Vlookup mapping formulas in each source, making Vlookup the less favorable
option in cases where your classifications apply to multiple data sources. Also, the Vlookup command is
only initiated for data that was active during the data stream’s processing period. For example, if the
classification data stream is updated with a new PO value for a certain Facebook campaign, and the
Facebook data stream is reprocessed for the last 10 days, the new PO value isn’t updated in the
Facebook data stream if this particular campaign wasn’t active within these last 10 days. Using Vlookup is
more time consuming and has a higher likelihood that certain data isn’t updated.
Competitive Data Stream Type
The competitive data stream type is suitable for datasets coming from public APIs and competitive
analysis tools. This data stream type is designed to fit multiple content worlds, all under the competitive
data category. It includes vendor-specific measurements like Facebook page and Twitter likes and
generic measurements suitable for multiple cases, such as lifetime likes.
The competitive data stream type allows you to view your competitors’ data separately from your own
data. For example, you’re the marketing manager for a university. Your social data is retrieved from
different vendors, including Facebook, and it’s mapped using the social data stream type. Your
competitor data is retrieved using public data providers like Facebook Public and it’s mapped using the
competitive data stream type. This way, the page likes for other universities aren’t mapped to the same
measurements as your university.
Because competitive and social data is retrieved from different vendors, it’s mapped to different data
stream types. Creating an identical view of your data and your competitors’ data can be tricky. Retrieve
your data from the public and competitive vendors that you’re using. This method makes comparing
your data to competitors’ data much easier.
Competitive Data Stream Type Entities
The competitive data stream type has three entities, with the competitive page entity being the data
stream type’s main entity.

CRM Data Stream Types
CRM stands for Customer Relationship Management. There are two data stream types for uploading
CRM data. One type for uploading leads data called CRM Leads, and one for opportunities data called
CRM. The CRM data stream type is equipped with slowly changing type 2 dimensions such as lead stage,
lead status, and lead modified date.
The CRM leads data stream type ingests data from lead generation platforms such as Marketing Cloud
93

=== PAGE 98 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Account Engagement (Pardot), Hubspot Marketing, and Intercom. It also ingests data from CRM
platforms such as Salesforce, Sugar CRM, and CRM Creation.
CRM data stream types are unique since they’re the only data stream types that have Slowly Changing
Type 2 Dimensions. They’re also the only data stream types that upload data at the workspace level,
rather than the data stream level.
Slowly Changing Dimensions Type 2
With CRM data, opportunity or lead attributes are updated over time because an opportunity can go
through different stages. The CRM leads data stream type uses the slowly changing dimensions type 2
method to enable a data display suitable for these changes in dimension values. After a dimension’s
value changes, the new value appears from the modification date and onwards. The value isn’t
updated retroactively.
Lead Stage Dimension
The Lead Stage, one of the attributes of the Lead Entity, is a type 2 dimension from the CRM leads data
stream type. Over its life-span, it evolves and changes until it hopefully converts to an Opportunity.
Storing CRM Data
The CRM data is stored in the database in two separate tables.
Lead Modified Data in the CRM Data Stream Type
The Lead Modified Date is mandatory and serves as the only date-type field in the CRM data stream
types. It allows you to put the data on a timeline or have it filtered by date.
Lead Status
The lead status value has direct implications on how the lead stage value is displayed over a selected
date range.
Differences Between Leads and Opportunity Data Stream Types
The difference between Leads and Opportunity data stream types is seen by their Type 2 dimensions.
Measurements in the CRM Data Stream Types
There are two system-generated measurements, one for each CRM data stream type: Leads Count,
and Opportunities Count. These measurements perform a distinct count at Lead Key and Opportunity
Key level. For example, if you have a lead with three stages, the Leads Count measurement returns the
result of one as opposed to three.
The Connection Between the CRM Data Stream Types
The CRM Leads and CRM Opportunities data stream types are connected by shared dimensions and
attributes.
Population of the Opportunity ID
The opportunity ID is populated for each lead that’s populated in the source data.
Visualizing CRM Data
There are different ways that you can visualize CRM data. The visualization of a type 2 dimension, such
as Lead Stage, filtered for a certain date range, returns the dimension’s updated value. This is
according to its last Lead Modified Date value contained within the filtered date range.
Row Count in CRM Data Stream Types
When a slowly changing type 2 dimension changes its value over time, each value along with its
Modified Date is stored on a separate row in the database, identified by a unique Detail ID.
94

=== PAGE 99 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Slowly Changing Dimensions Type 2
With CRM data, opportunity or lead attributes are updated over time because an opportunity can go
through different stages. The CRM leads data stream type uses the slowly changing dimensions type 2
method to enable a data display suitable for these changes in dimension values. After a dimension’s
value changes, the new value appears from the modification date and onwards. The value isn’t updated
retroactively.
After a dimension’s value changes, the new value appears from the modification date and onwards. The
value isn’t updated retroactively.
The CRM data stream type includes four dimensions that work according to the slowly changing
dimensions type 2 method:
•
Status
•
Stage
•
Modified Date
•
Expected Close Date
For the platform to display the data properly, you must map two crucial dimensions:
•
Modified Date - This field is mandatory. It serves as the only date-type field in this data stream type.
The lead modified date is the date on which the other type 2 dimensions lead stage, and lead status,
changed.
•
Opportunity Status – This field has a closed list of values. The Opportunity Status field must be
mapped and contain one of these values. If the Opportunity Status isn’t mapped or it’s mapped
incorrectly, it can cause discrepancies in your data. The platform refers to any value that isn’t one of
the values, as CLOSED.
- OPEN – The opportunity is ongoing.
- CLOSED – The opportunity is resolved. After an opportunity is classified as CLOSED it doesn’t appear
in dates past its last modified date.
- DELETED – The opportunity isn’t relevant, the platform doesn’t display any appearances of the
opportunity in the data. Because data from different platforms can contain different values, use a
formula to map the Opportunity Status Dimension, for example:
- IF(csv['status'] contains 'Open', 'OPEN', 'CLOSED')
Or if you’re extracting the Opportunity Status from the Stage field:
- IF(csv['stage'] contains 'Closed', 'CLOSED', 'OPEN')
95

=== PAGE 100 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Lead Stage Dimension
The Lead Stage, one of the attributes of the Lead Entity, is a type 2 dimension from the CRM leads data
stream type. Over its life-span, it evolves and changes until it hopefully converts to an Opportunity.
Each of the new Stage values and the Dates on which these changes have taken place is what constitutes
the history of its attribute values:
Unlike the behavior of type 1 dimensions that can only hold one value (per Entity Key), a type 2
dimension can retain a full history of its attribute values due to how it’s stored in the database. Each Lead
Key is broken-down into additional rows. Each row holds the new Lead Stage value alongside the date on
which this change occurred, the Modified Date. These additional rows are identified by a unique Lead
Detail ID. It’s the same Lead Key over multiple rows with different Lead Detail IDs:
Note The Lead Detail ID can’t be accessed from within the platform, that is, you can’t select the
Lead Detail ID from within a widget. For example, when you want to see all the stage values of a
certain Lead Key. Its use is purely internal for the purpose of keeping a history of attribute values as
previously described.
Querying your data for a certain date range always returns the last updated Stage value for the last
Modified Date within the selected date range, not a list of all the values leading up to the last Modified
Date. To use the earlier dataset as an example, querying for Lead Key 135711 from 1/1/2019 to 22/1/
2019 doesn’t return 3 values: 'New', 'Working' and 'Nurturing', but rather only the updated (last) value
for that date range, for example,‘Nurturing’.
Storing CRM Data
The CRM data is stored in the database in two separate tables.
96

=== PAGE 101 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
One table has the Lead Key, Lead Detail IDs, and Type 2 Dimensions.
And the other table has Lead Key with the Type 1 Dimensions:
You can examine the way in which your CRM data is stored in the database using the Dimension
Explorer. Here you can see that the CRM entities have two identical fields accessible from the Dimension
Explorer’s dropdown. Each entity is sourced from a different table.
By viewing the dimensions available in each table allows you to recognize which table they belong to.
97

=== PAGE 102 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Having access to the CRM data from the Dimension Explorer and understanding its allocation into the
separate tables allows you to examine and manage your CRM data better. For example, the Dimension
Explorer is the only place from which you can see the data at Lead Detail ID level. It’s where you can
validate the different Stage values and Modified Dates for a certain Lead, as it appears in the database.
Since the data is at the workspace level, if you must delete your data, you have to delete the relevant
rows from the Dimension Explorer. Deleting the related data stream doesn’t delete the data.
When deleting CRM data, it must be deleted from both tables. If you delete the data from the table that
contains the Key and Type 1 Dimensions, then the data from the table with the Detail ID breakdown is
automatically deleted .
Lead Modified Data in the CRM Data Stream Type
The Lead Modified Date is mandatory and serves as the only date-type field in the CRM data stream
types. It allows you to put the data on a timeline or have it filtered by date.
98

=== PAGE 103 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The Lead Modified Date is the date on which the other type 2 dimensions within this data stream type,
Lead Stage, and Lead Status, have changed their values.
Note The Lead Modified Date dimension is strictly associated with these two dimensions. If for
example a certain type 1 attribute such as Lead Source changes, this new Lead Source value isn’t
associated with a Lead Modified Date.
Since there are only Modified Date values for when the type 2 dimensions change their values, this
produces a ‘data gap’ for any date values other than those specific Modified Date values. For example, in
the Stage Timeline, if you query the data between 15/1/2019 and 19/1/2019, you don’t have any data
for this date range:
The query fills in the data gap by creating Modified Date values between the two available Modified Date
values. It stretches or duplicates the first Stage value across that date range, thus creating data continuity.
This allows for data to match the query, as shown below:
99

=== PAGE 104 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Note The first part of the Lead lifecycle (between creation and first stage) is more sensitive than the
other stages, since its accuracy depends on whether all the historic data is pulled in. For example, if
data is pulled in from the Jan 16, 2019 onwards, any Lead Stage changes that have taken place
before the 16th are unknown, because that data isn’t being pulled. In such a case, stretching the
‘Working’ value all the way back to the Jan 13, 2019 may not be true for some of the dates before
Jan 16, 2019. If you need accurate historical data from when the Lead was first created, make sure
you pull in the historical data accordingly.
This method carries through for all the other data gaps; however from the first stage onwards, the
Modified Dates, and Stage values are stretched forward as opposed to backwards. So the ‘Working’ Stage
value is stretched across from the Jan 23rd until one day prior to the date on which it changes again for
example, until January 23rd. On the 27th it changes again to ‘Nurturing’ which is true until the Jan 30th
and so on.
Lead Status
The lead status value has direct implications on how the lead stage value is displayed over a selected
date range.
The Lead Status can be one of the following three values:
100

=== PAGE 105 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
•
UNQUALIFIED
•
QUALIFIED
•
DELETED
Unqualified means that the lead’s potential hasn’t been fully actualized even after its last available Stage
value on its Modified Date. Querying for such a lead for a date range that exceeds the lead’s last Modified
Date still returns the last Stage value despite there not being any Stage value for that date range. For
example, consider a lead whose last Modified Date for its ‘Nurturing’ Stage is Jan 31, 2019. Querying this
lead’s data for a date range that relates to a much later period, Dec 1, 2019 to Dec 31, 2019, still returns
the ‘Nurturing’ value:
This is achieved with the query assigning the last Stage value, in this case ‘Nurturing’, from the last
Modified Date indefinitely:
Qualified means that a lead’s potential had been fully actualized. As such, querying for such a lead for a
date range that exceeds the lead’s last Modified Date returns no data. For example, consider a lead
whose last Modified Date for its ‘Nurturing’ Stage is Jan 31, 2019. Querying this lead’s data from
February 1, 2019 onwards returns no data:
101

=== PAGE 106 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Deleted means that the lead is no longer relevant. Querying the data for such a lead returns no data,
irrespective of the date range.
Differences Between Leads and Opportunity Data Stream Types
The difference between Leads and Opportunity data stream types is seen by their Type 2 dimensions.
Both CRM data stream types have the same Type 2 dimensions with the exact same behavior. The
Opportunity data stream type has an additional Type 2 dimension called Opportunity Expected Close
Date. This attribute is another opportunity attribute whose value changes over time as part of the sales
process.
Another difference is that the Opportunities data stream type also allows for measurements to be
mapped, whereas the Leads data stream type doesn’t.
Measurements in the CRM Data Stream Types
There are two system-generated measurements, one for each CRM data stream type: Leads Count, and
Opportunities Count. These measurements perform a distinct count at Lead Key and Opportunity Key
level. For example, if you have a lead with three stages, the Leads Count measurement returns the result
of one as opposed to three.
The Opportunities data stream type supports mapping measurements. However, it’s recommended you
use system-generated measurements to create any required calculations internally, as opposed to pulling
102

=== PAGE 107 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
them directly from the source.
The Connection Between the CRM Data Stream Types
The CRM Leads and CRM Opportunities data stream types are connected by shared dimensions and
attributes.
Within the CRM Leads data stream type there’s a dimension called ‘Lead Converted Opportunity Key’ to
which you’re supposed to map the Opportunity ID associated with the lead in question:
If the Lead Converted Opportunity Key is mapped in the CRM Leads data stream type, data fusion is
applied automatically behind the scenes between the Lead Converted Opportunity Key and the
Opportunity Key from the CRM (Opportunities) data stream type:
After data fusion is established, data is consolidated from both sources for any matching Key values.
This fusion takes place behind the scenes and can’t be accessed from the platform. Furthermore,
because there can only be one fusion between two separate data stream types, you can’t create your
own fusion between these two data stream types.
103

=== PAGE 108 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Population of the Opportunity ID
The opportunity ID is populated for each lead that’s populated in the source data.
In Salesforce for example, if upon Lead conversion the appropriate ‘Convert Lead’ button is pressed (as
shown below), then this field is automatically populated. You still must make sure that you include this
field in your data stream query so that it’s included in the returned result. You can then map it to the
Lead Converted Opportunity Key appropriately.
If this procedure isn’t regularly followed within Salesforce, you have to pull this dimension value from
elsewhere (perhaps using another field or even perhaps another table within Salesforce). Otherwise the
fusion can’t work without the Lead Converted Opportunity Key being mapped.
When looking at fused or connected data for Leads and Opportunities, expect to see ‘gaps’, for example,
leads or opportunities, which don’t seem to be connected to anything. For example, if you have leads
that haven’t matured into opportunities yet, or opportunities that never started off as leads in the first
place. In both cases, the leads and opportunities don’t have matching keys and the fusion doesn’t work,
leaving these items to appear as gaps in a fused display.

There are different ways that you can visualize CRM data. The visualization of a type 2 dimension, such as
Lead Stage, filtered for a certain date range, returns the dimension’s updated value. This is according to
its last Lead Modified Date value contained within the filtered date range.

Best Practices
If you’re uploading Salesforce data, use the Salesforce SOQL connector.
•
Using this method is unlimited in terms of the number of rows that are returned by the query, as
opposed to using the Salesforce Reports Connector, which is limited to 2,000 rows.
•
As a ‘healthy’ setup, you should create one data stream for pulling the 'Historic' data from the CRM
source and one data stream for updating ‘Deltas’ (changes). With the data being updated at the
workspace level, this setup is easy to manage and maintain:
•
Avoid using the TODAY() date function for ‘Modified Date’. This is bad practice since it increases row
count and affects performance. Since hourly breakdown isn’t supported, applying TODAY() means you
could potentially lose data, as there can’t be more than one row for the same lead for the same date.
106

=== PAGE 111 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Row Count in CRM Data Stream Types
When a slowly changing type 2 dimension changes its value over time, each value along with its Modified
Date is stored on a separate row in the database, identified by a unique Detail ID.
The same Lead Key for example, can have multiple Lead Detail ID rows. With the CRM data stream types,
the row count is summed up as per the number of Detail ID rows generated in the database, regardless
of the number of lead or opportunity keys themselves. So, the same Lead Key for example, with 5
different stage values on separate Lead Modified Dates, is counted as 5 rows.
CRM Data Stream Structures
Note The "Country" dimension is an attribute of the "Company key" dimension. If you don’t map
the Company key and have different country values, only one country appears.

The data stream jobs data stream type allows you to view statistics and information relevant to the
execution of your data streams. The data included in this data stream type is the same as the data
displayed in the data stream's log.
The data displayed is per file uploaded to TotalConnect or per run when discussing API data streams. To
view the data aggregated at the data stream level, you can use dimensions and measurements from the
data stream data stream type.

Ecommerce Data Stream Type
The ecommerce data stream type ingests different data types from ecommerce platforms. It provides
levels of data for product catalogs, placed orders, number of purchased items within each order, and
more.
The ecommerce data stream type is an extension to the product data stream type. It accommodates
more use cases and datasets revolving around ecommerce data. You can use it for any flat file that
contains ecommerce data related to orders, order items, and products. It’s also the data stream type that
the ecommerce API connectors Amazon Seller Central, Amazon Vendor Central Inventory, and Salesforce
Order Management (OMS) use.
The ecommerce data stream type provides this information.
•
Product catalog—Tracks and analyzes products that you have in different ecommerce sources. You can
tie it to the revenue for each product and use it to remove the granularity of order level.
•
Order level data—Tracks details about orders placed over time, including revenue, tax, quantity ordered
and shipped, and order level discounts.
•
Order item level data—Applies to each product purchased in a specific order. It includes the product
level data and price on the date of purchase. You link it to the Product Catalog to fill the data of each
item purchased, such as image, category, and SKU.
Use Cases
Here are some common use cases for the ecommerce data stream type.
•
Track and investigate measurements at the order level: AOV, total revenue, ROAS, #number of orders
Map the order level data, with or without the product or order item level data.
•
Track and investigate product KPIs: Top performing products, product performance over time
Map product level data that contains revenue to the order total revenue field. An order key is created
so that you can map the data easily according to the time period revenue for each product.
•
Track ROAS at a campaign and product level: product purchases generated by campaigns
Campaign level data is attached to each order with web analytics tracking, using the Order Key as a
link. Each campaign is then mapped to the order items to understand which products were purchased
for each campaign that ran. When using the ecommerce data stream type, discounts, taxes, and order
cancellations are considered to calculate a more accurate ROAS.

Mapping Advertiser Data to Campaign Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Campaign data isn’t connected to advertiser data, so you can’t see campaign data with the
ecommerce data stream type. Instead, you can manually map your campaign data to either the order
level or order item (SKU) level.
Reducing the Number of Rows Produced with the Ecommerce Data Stream Type
Ecommerce data contains different levels, from product to order level data. As a result, it can include a
large number of rows to map to get the desired insights. (For example, you can have a woman’s dress
in three colors and three sizes). When viewing this data, each level has several rows. To reduce the
number of rows, you can aggregate the data on a less granular level using these options.

Mapping Advertiser Data to Campaign Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Campaign data isn’t connected to advertiser data, so you can’t see campaign data with the ecommerce
data stream type. Instead, you can manually map your campaign data to either the order level or order
item (SKU) level.
Connecting the campaign at the order level connects all product purchases generated from a specific
campaign. You can also see which campaigns generated sales for products that weren’t advertised.
Connecting the campaign at the SKU level connects each individual product purchase with the
campaign, for campaigns that were product-specific.
Connecting the advertising data together with ecommerce data allows calculation of ROAS (Return on
Advertising Spend) per product.
To manually map your campaign data:
•
Map Campaign Name or Campaign Key to the Order Key or Order Item Key.
•
Create a pattern or classification rule to harmonize the name of the campaigns to the names of the
products, or the product categories that the campaigns are running on.
If you have a campaign for specific products, use naming conventions to extract the product name.
113

=== PAGE 118 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Reducing the Number of Rows Produced with the Ecommerce Data Stream Type
Ecommerce data contains different levels, from product to order level data. As a result, it can include a
large number of rows to map to get the desired insights. (For example, you can have a woman’s dress in
three colors and three sizes). When viewing this data, each level has several rows. To reduce the number
of rows, you can aggregate the data on a less granular level using these options.
•
Map your data at the SKU (product) level directly to the order item data model to show revenue
brought from products while removing the granularity level of the orders.
•
Map your data at the product category level directly to the order item data model, showing revenue
on the category level.
•
Aggregate your data with the Granular Data Center, create queries, and then use TotalConnect to pull
it into Intelligence with any granularity level that you choose. For more information about the Granular
Data Center, contact your Marketing Cloud Intelligence representative.
Ecommerce Data Stream Type Considerations
Keep these considerations in mind when using the ecommerce data stream type.
•
Because the ecommerce data stream type includes the product data stream type, you no longer need
to use the product data stream type.
•
Your data from the product data stream type is migrated for you. It’s recommended that you change
the mapping of Revenue to either Order Total Revenue or Order Item Revenue to have one revenue
field for all future connectors.
•
You can connect your ecommerce data with your advertising data using the Campaign Name field at
either the order level data or order item level data.
•
If your ecommerce data contains a large number of rows, map your data to the product level or
category level. You can also use Granular Data Centers to pull in granular data, query it, and then
import using TotalConnect.
Uploading New Data into an Ecommerce Data Stream
What happens to ecommerce data after it’s been uploaded to Marketing Cloud Intelligence and new
data is retrieved? When is the data stream performing an upsert as opposed to a replace? The
ecommerce data stream type acts differently from other data stream types.
The ecommerce data stream type pulls all of its data based on three entities: product, order, and order
item (which represents a product in a specific order). When new data is loaded, there are two types of
data.
Data containing the order key
The order key is from the data stream. If the status, revenue, or any other dimension or metric
was updated, Marketing Cloud Intelligence recognizes that the order already exists and overrides
the revenue and other properties based on the order key. For example, if an order updates the
status from created to shipped, the status updates according to the update date. The created
114

=== PAGE 119 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Data Model for Marketing Cloud Intelligence
date is saved as a different property.
Data not containing the order key
This data is updated daily or on a regular basis but doesn’t have a specific order key, such as
product sales per day. The data model creates an order key behind the scenes according to the
data stream and the date and time of data retrieval. If data is updated on the same day, then the
same order key is generated according to the data and the data stream’s ID and all the data is
replaced accordingly.
Note Because this behavior is dependent on the data being loaded, the Upsert and Replace options
in the advanced settings aren’t relevant for the ecommerce data stream type.
Generic Data Stream Type in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The generic data stream type ingests data that doesn’t comply with any of the existing Marketing Cloud
Intelligence data models. All entities in a generic data stream type are in a many-to-many relationship. It
offers 50 custom attributes for each entity and 100 custom measurements. The generic data stream type
is flexible and caters to a wide range of data types.
Here’s a video about the generic data stream type:
Watch the video: https://salesforce.vidyard.com/watch/fUfcBPqwjPm1fNtW74kJ5x?
Generic Data Stream Type Entities and Measurements
The generic data steam type contains five entities and one hundred custom measurements.
Create a Generic Data Stream
Create a generic data stream when you must upload data that doesn’t comply with any of the existing
Marketing Cloud Intelligence data models. For example, create a generic data stream if you have
survey data that you want to visualize but it doesn’t fit into any of the preset data stream types.
Generic Data Stream Type Entities and Measurements
The generic data steam type contains five entities and one hundred custom measurements.
Entities — The generic data stream type has five entities: one main entity and four peripheral entities. The
main entity is always named after the data stream created. The entities have a many-to-many
relationship, and each entity has fifty custom attributes.
Measurements — Generic data stream types have one hundred custom measurements. Although generic
data stream types have a unique structure, they’re still an integral part of the Marketing Cloud
Intelligence data model. The main entity maintains a relationship with the overarching entities.

Messaging Data Stream Type in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The messaging data stream type ingests email, mobile phone, and journey data. A message can be in the
form of an email, text message, or app notification. It’s typically associated with measurements such as
how many people received, opened, or clicked a message. This data stream type can be used to ingest
data from platforms such as Salesforce Marketing Cloud, Marketo, and Adobe.
Messaging Data Stream Type Entities in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The messaging data stream type contains three types of entities: messaging, ads, and overarching.
Messaging Data Stream Type Entities in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The messaging data stream type contains three types of entities: messaging, ads, and overarching.
Message Entities
Message Send is this data stream type’s main entity. All entities and measurements primarily
relate to the message send. For example, the recipient list is the name of the list that a message
send was sent to. Similarly, the journey is the journey or a workflow that a message send is a part
of, and so on.
Ads Entities
Ads entities are entities used in Ads-related data stream types. They enable linking messaging
data to ads data based on matching dimension values for campaign, site, and media buy. For
example, if you have a matching campaign name in a messaging and ads data stream, you can
see the message open rate and the impressions that the same campaign generates in each
source.
Overarching Entities
Overarching entities are classification-purpose dimensions that exist in all data stream types and
enable classifying data from multiple sources for matching values. For example, it’s possible to
see all the data from messaging and analytics data stream types for the matching product value.
The hierarchical relationship between entities can be a one-to-many or a many-to-many
relationship. This relationship dictates the number of possible values of one entity against the
other. For example, the relationship between product and message send is a one-to-many
relationship. One product can have many message sends, but each message send can only have
one product.
117

=== PAGE 122 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Messaging Measurements
This table lists all the measurements in the messaging data stream type alongside their aggregation
method.
This data stream type has the exact same measurements in both Sum Aggregation and Lifetime
Aggregation. For example, message soft bounces from the source data can be mapped to message soft
bounces or message soft bounces (Lifetime) in Marketing Cloud Intelligence. You can decide which
aggregation method to use based on the relationship between the date breakdown in the source data.
The messaging data stream type also contains Rate Measurements in percentage terms.
Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
One of the most crucial steps when working with Marketing Cloud Intelligence, is uploading data. Since
Marketing Cloud Intelligence supports numerous types of data, you can choose from many different
ways to upload it. During the upload stage, you can map your data to determine which fields appear in
Marketing Cloud Intelligence, and you can even create custom calculated fields to get the most out of
your data. All these actions are done in the Connect & Mix tab.
Create Data Streams in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Whenever you upload data, data streams are created. These data streams can be referenced
throughout the Marketing Cloud Intelligence platform, for example in pivot tables, reports, and
dashboards. There are several methods available for creating data streams in Marketing Cloud
Intelligence. You can use an API connector to connect via API, TotalConnect to upload external files,
LiteConnect if you have data from a single source, or even Granular Data Centers if you have granular
data. You can choose which method is best for you.
Mapping Your Source Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Mapping connects the source fields in your uploaded file to the corresponding Marketing Cloud
Intelligence fields. Intelligence uses machine learning algorithms to map your data. The mapping
process is similar regardless of which method you use to upload your data, with some minor
differences. If you’re using an API connector to upload your data, Marketing Cloud Intelligence
automatically maps the fields for you, but you can make changes to the mapping. If you’re using
TotalConnect, Intelligence identifies the most suitable data stream type according to your data and
offers an initial mapping suggestion.
Advanced Settings in Data Streams in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Define advanced settings to your data stream to select load modes, load rules, or configure additional
settings. The available settings vary based on the type of data stream that you’re creating.
Data Stream Control Center
The Data Stream Control Center is a dashboard that centralizes data stream management into a single
118

=== PAGE 123 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
view. You can assess stream status, evaluate data quality, and identify ingestion issues. Get actionable
insights for improved data hygiene and row management. The Data Stream Control Center is
automatically filtered to show data for Today. To view the Data Stream Control Center dashboard, in
the Connect & Mix tab, expand the Control Center tab.
Data Stream List
View, manage, and create data streams from the Data Stream List, in the Connect & Mix tab.
Data Stream Templates in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Use data stream templates to create a mapping template that you can replicate to create data
streams. Store the template on the workspace or account level. You can share data stream templates
between workspaces and accounts. The data stream template saves formulas used in the original data
stream.
Data Mapping Visualizer
Use the Data Mapping Visualizer tool to view an animated display of the mapped data in your
Intelligence workspace. Data Mapping Visualizer lets you easily view and search your mapped data.
Workflows in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Use workflows to execute data streams, reports, and actions in your workspace. For example, create a
workflow to generate a report after specific data streams are processed.
Transformation Rules
Use transformation rules to set values to a specified attribute, based on a set of defined criteria. The
rules can be set at the level of the entity key or name. Transformation rules are most effective when
the entity key or name has a consistent naming convention.
Transformation Groups
Transformation groups allow you to classify attributes of entities, such as sites, campaigns, or
products, so you can filter information.
Create Data Streams in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Whenever you upload data, data streams are created. These data streams can be referenced throughout
the Marketing Cloud Intelligence platform, for example in pivot tables, reports, and dashboards. There
are several methods available for creating data streams in Marketing Cloud Intelligence. You can use an
API connector to connect via API, TotalConnect to upload external files, LiteConnect if you have data
from a single source, or even Granular Data Centers if you have granular data. You can choose which
method is best for you.
Creating Data Streams with TotalConnect in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
If you need to upload data from a source and no API connector exists, you can create a data stream
with TotalConnect. For example, if you have a proprietary dataset in Excel, CSV, or another file format,
or if a custom report is sent to you by a data partner that is beyond the scope of what their API can
provide.
Creating Data Streams with API Connectors in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
API connectors enable you to easily connect data sources such as Google Ads or Facebook to
Marketing Cloud Intelligence. When you connect an API connector, an API data stream is created that
automatically maps the data fields the vendor provides to the Marketing Cloud Intelligence data
119

=== PAGE 124 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
model, allowing you to access your information.
Creating Data Streams with LiteConnect in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
With LiteConnect, you can upload a file into Marketing Cloud Intelligence as is and visualize the data
automatically. You don’t need to consider the predefined structure and constraints of the data model
or map fields.
Create Data Streams with DirectConnect
DirectConnect is a premium feature that allows you to retrieve data directly from an external database,
without having to store the data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence’s database. DirectConnect is used in
cases where you have data sensitivity issues, or you want to display and interact with your data from an
external source instead.
Pull Data 360 Data into Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Data 360 unifies your data from multiple sources so that you can build a comprehensive view of your
customer. Pull in a small amount of data with TotalConnect or try the Granular Data Center connector
for larger amounts. You can also install the Data Cloud app to get predefined insights.
Creating Data Streams with TotalConnect in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
If you need to upload data from a source and no API connector exists, you can create a data stream with
TotalConnect. For example, if you have a proprietary dataset in Excel, CSV, or another file format, or if a
custom report is sent to you by a data partner that is beyond the scope of what their API can provide.
With TotalConnect, you can either upload a flat file or use a technical vendor. You can define the retrieval
method, for example, if you want to pull your data via an HTTPS server. You can also decide what to do
when new data comes in, for example, replace the existing data with the new data or filter out data
before it’s loaded.
Create a Data Stream with TotalConnect in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
You can upload data manually, automatically using FTP, or an email address, or by scheduling an
update with a technical vendor.
Preview Source Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
After you upload your data with TotalConnect, a preview of your source data lets you see how
Marketing Cloud Intelligence reads the raw data, including fields and their types. The table reflects all
the fields (columns) from your source file. Each field is defined with a type—text, number, or date—and
you change the type if needed. You can also change the date format.
Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Transformers are a set of configurations applied to files that are uploaded into Marketing Cloud
Intelligence. Transformers are used to alter the structure of the data files in order to pull the exact data
sets you need. They instruct the platform as to what data table must be extracted from your source file
allowing you to manipulate the file structure.
Mapping Your Source Data in TotalConnect
Mapping connects the source fields in your uploaded file to the corresponding Marketing Cloud
Intelligence fields. Marketing Cloud Intelligence uses machine learning algorithms to map your data.
The mapping process is similar regardless of which method you use to upload your data, with some
minor differences. If you’re using TotalConnect, Intelligence identifies the most suitable data stream

Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
type according to your data and offers an initial mapping suggestion.
Troubleshoot Total Connect Data Streams
Errors can occur that prevent data streams from being created or processed. You can view errors using
the Log button. The log contains important technical information and can help you diagnose issues.

You can upload data manually, automatically using FTP, or an email address, or by scheduling an update
with a technical vendor.

Field Types
Each field is defined with a different type (text, number, or date), and you can change the type if
121

=== PAGE 126 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
necessary. Click the column name and select the type.
Date Type Fields
When it comes to date type fields, recognizing date formats is of utmost importance regarding data
correctness and aggregation. In order to ingest a particular row of data, Marketing Cloud Intelligence
must be able to read the date format correctly.
Since there are a variety of different date formats, Marketing Cloud Intelligence automatically recognizes
the format that was used in the data source. In some cases, multiple formats are used, therefore more
than one format is found.
To change the date format or add a second date format, click the Day column and then click the Settings
icon. You can select a format from the list or enter a date format. To apply more than two date formats,
use the PARSEDATE() function.
Transformers
Transformers are applied to your source file to create the final data table that is used later when
mapping. Four transformers are automatically applied: File, Table, Headers, and Totals. You can add
transformers for more advanced file structure manipulations.
When you apply transformers, you can see how your source data is affected in Preview.
See Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence for more information about what you can do with
Transformers.
Highlight Tool
Use the highlight tool to focus on a specific field type. For example, you can highlight all the date
columns.
You can highlight date, text, and number fields, as well as columns added by transformers, which create
additional columns, like transpose, or unnest.
Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Transformers are a set of configurations applied to files that are uploaded into Marketing Cloud
Intelligence. Transformers are used to alter the structure of the data files in order to pull the exact data
sets you need. They instruct the platform as to what data table must be extracted from your source file
122

=== PAGE 127 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
allowing you to manipulate the file structure.
For example, if your source file contains extra or missing information— missing headers, multiple sheets,
and extra rows or columns. The transformers allow you to manipulate the file structure, for example
inserting a default header where it’s missing so that data is pulled correctly. Transformers are applied
during the preview source step when creating or editing a TotalConnect data stream. Four transformers
are applied to your data automatically: File, Table, Headers, and Totals. Add additional transformers for
more advanced file structure manipulations.
Apply File Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Use file transformers when you want to transform a file that has special characteristics like uncommon
delimiters, multiple sheets, or specific file encoding. For example, if a file uses ~~ as the delimiter
instead of a comma or a tab, use the file transformer to specify the custom delimiter so that you can
map the file correctly.
Apply Table Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The table transformer enables you to customize a table’s start and end rows and columns.
Apply Header Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
If you want to customize the header structure when headers aren’t available or span multiple rows,
you can use a header transformer. For example, if a file has a two-line header that must be combined
into one row.
Total Rows Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Use the total rows transformer when you want to change the default behavior of Marketing Cloud
Intelligence to strip out “totals” rows from all files. For example, if a file has non-additive
measurements, and you want only the totals row mapped, use this transformer.
Transpose Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Use a transpose transformer when you want to reconstruct a flat-file format by translating columns of
data into rows. For example, if a file has three conversion types in three separate columns that you
want to aggregate into “total conversions,” they must be transposed into rows from columns. A
column of data in a flat-file corresponds to a field that could be mapped in the Marketing Cloud
Intelligence data model. So anytime you can streamline the number of potential mapped fields, you
free up fields that can be used for other dimensions or measurements. After transpose settings are set
for a data stream, new files uploaded to the data stream automatically inherit the predefined pattern
that was specified upon initial integration.
Apply Unnest Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The unnest transformer enables you to transform concatenated values into separate rows. The
measurement values linked to the nested row are duplicated to each new row. For example, If there
are three values, comma-separated, and one field must be broken out into individual rows of data, this
transformer creates individual rows for each value in that field.
Apply Pivot Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
The pivot transformer can transform values from selected columns into new individual columns, which
then contain aggregated values for the measurements.
Apply PGP/GPG Decryptor Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
You can use the PGP/GPG transformer when you want to decrypt uploaded encrypted files with a
PGP/GPG decrypting tool.

Apply File Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Use file transformers when you want to transform a file that has special characteristics like uncommon
delimiters, multiple sheets, or specific file encoding. For example, if a file uses ~~ as the delimiter instead
of a comma or a tab, use the file transformer to specify the custom delimiter so that you can map the file
correctly.

CSV Options
•
Use Unquoted Multiline Fields Format — The standard in text files is that quotation marks surround
each value. For example, "campaign name". The quotation marks indicate where the value starts and
ends. When “Enter” is used, it marks a new line of data. When these settings don’t exist, you need a
special configuration so that the platform understands the file correctly. Before selecting this option,
it’s advisable to consult with the R&D team.
•
Use Custom Delimiter — All text files have a delimiter that separates between different columns. Two
of the more common separators are CSV, which uses a comma-separated file, and TSV, which uses a
tab-separated file. You can also create a text file that has a special separator. Typically you use this
option when there are tabs and commas inside the data.
•
Use Custom Quote Character — The standard in text files is that quotation marks surround. For
example, "campaign name". The quotation marks indicate where the value starts and ends. In some
cases, there’s no separator between values. When quotation marks or separators don’t exist, you need
a special configuration so that the platform understands the file correctly.
124

=== PAGE 129 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
•
Use Custom Escape Character — An escape character allows you to insert characters that are usually
part of a file’s string and not as standalone characters. In text files, the standard escape character is
usually a backslash \. For example, you have inches in your file, say, 21", and your quote character is ".
The escape character allows the platform to understand that the quotes aren’t used as quote
characters, but rather as part of the data. So if you enter 21\", the data looks like 21" at the end.
However, if your file contains a different escape character, other than a backslash, select this option,
and then insert the custom escape character that’s in your file.
•
File Encoding — It’s recommended that you set this option to Auto Detect. In cases where you see that
the encoding wasn’t properly detected, for example, the field names read like gibberish or are sliced
incorrectly, set the encoding manually.
Excel Options
•
Sheet Name — If you have multiple sheets in your file, select the sheet that you want to upload. Or you
can use a wildcard with an asterisk for the sheet name in case it changes. For example, the sheet
contains today’s date, such as Report Jan 1, and the date changes with every upload (Report Feb 2,
Report Mar 3, etc). In that case, you can use a wildcard (Report*) and then choose the sheet
dynamically.
•
Use first sheet as default — Select this option, if you want the first sheet to be selected in cases where
the selected sheet isn’t found in the file.
•
Protected Workbook Password — If the Excel file you uploaded is password-protected, enter the
password here. Upload the file after the password is provided. If you didn’t know the file was
encrypted, and you uploaded it before being asked to supply a password, upload it again after
providing the password. Decryption is available only for workbook encryption for Excel version 2007
and above. Sheet encryption isn’t supported.
•
Best practice — Marketing Cloud Intelligence must be able to open the source file of the data stream,
which is the first file uploaded. After the source file is encrypted, you want the password for the data
stream to always match this file so that it can always be opened. When using passwords in encrypted
files the password can’t be changed. If you know the password could change over time, it’s best to
upload the first file in a non-protected format, and then all files after can be encrypted. If the
password changes over time, change it in the file transformers before uploading the file with the new
password.
XML Options
•
RSS Feed Format — If the file you’re uploading is in an RSS format, select this option.
•
RSS Custom Header Name — When you don’t want to use the standard RSS headers, enter the desired
headers in this field.
•
XML XSL Transformation — If you have XSL for the XML file that you’re uploading, enter it here. XSL is a
scripting language used to access XML elements. XSL is often used to reorganize the file’s structure.
You want your XSL script to reorganize the XML into a comma-delimited format, similar to that of a
CSV file. For more information on XSL functions and syntax, refer to this tutorial.
PDF Options
•
Pages — Insert the pages that you want to use in the file transformers. You can insert ranges separated
by a comma or all. For example, 1-3,5-7,9 or all.
125

=== PAGE 130 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
•
Protected Workbook — If your PDF is password-protected, insert the password here, and then upload
the file again to process it.
•
Page Area — Insert the portion of the page to analyze, such as top, left, bottom, or right. For example,
269.875,12.75,561.
•
Use Spreadsheet Mode — To force the PDF to be extracted using spreadsheet-style extraction, select
this option.
•
Use Non-Spreadsheet Mode — To force the PDF to not be extracted using spreadsheet-style
extraction, select this option.
JSON Options
•
Use JSON Path — When uploading a JSON file that includes hierarchies, you can use a JSON path to
access the specific hierarchy that you want to upload. For an explanation on the JSON path, refer to
this documentation.
•
Use Keys as Values — The default behavior when uploading a JSON file is to use the keys as the
column names. The key values appear in the Source Fields section. When this option is selected, the
keys are counted as values and the columns receive a name from Marketing Cloud Intelligence.
Example:
JSON file -
{
"id": 1, 2
"name": [“A green door", “window frame”]
"price": [12.50, 10.50]
"tags": [“home", “home”]
}
Default Behavior
id name tags
1 a green door home
2 window frame home
With 'Use Keys as Values" Checked
Header 1 Header 2 Header 3
id 1 2
name a green door window frame
price 12.5 10.5
tags home home
•
Flatten Nested Objects — When uploading a JSON file with hierarchies, Marketing Cloud Intelligence
recognizes only the top hierarchy by default. If you want to process lower hierarchies, you must flatten
the file. When the Flatten Nested Objects option is selected, the platform flattens the nested objects
and creates additional columns for them. It’s similar to the action performed by JSON to CSV tools.
126

(Currently, nested arrays aren’t supported.)

The table transformer enables you to customize a table’s start and end rows and columns.
RREEQQUUIIRREEDD EEDDIITTIIOONNSS

Example:
This file is several tables positioned one under the other. In this case, when uploading the file, the
platform recognizes the first table. If you want to create a data stream based on one of the lower tables,
for example, the table titled “Spring 2016”, you must use the table transformer.
Another example:
The data in this file is spread across the columns. In this case, the platform sometimes recognizes the

11.. From the Connect & Mix tab, select Data Streams, and then Data Streams List.

You can define the table’s boundaries by defining the start and end of the table (rows or columns) or by
using an expression.
•
To define the table’s start and end:
11..
In the Table Start At section, set the table’s start rows and columns. The starting row and column
can be set according to row and column number. The count starts at zero, meaning the first row of
the file is row number 0 and the first column is column number 0.
22..
If you want the platform to automatically recognize the start row or column, use the Auto settings.
If set to auto, the platform uses the regular algorithm to recognize the data placement.
33..
In the Table End At section, use one of these options:
•• - Find Automatically — Find the table’s end using the platform's algorithm.
•• - After Identifying Rows in the Table — Find the table’s end according to a set number of rows or
127

=== PAGE 132 ===
Marketing Cloud Intelligence Uploading Data in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
columns.
•• - After Identifying Empty Rows — Find the table’s end according to the number of empty rows after
the last row.
•
To define the table’s start or end with an expression:
This is appropriate when the table’s location in the file changes from upload to upload. The relevant
variables for these expressions are:
- csv['row'] — Returns the array of each row’s values. You can specify the column by using [index]. For
example, csv['row'][0] returns the value of the first cell for each row, and csv['row'][1] returns the
value of the second cell for each row, etc.
- csv['prev_row'] — Returns the array of values of the previous row relative to csv['row'].
- csv['row_non_empty_cells'] — Returns the number of non-empty cells found in each csv['row'].

Total Rows Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Use the total rows transformer when you want to change the default behavior of Marketing Cloud
Intelligence to strip out “totals” rows from all files. For example, if a file has non-additive measurements,
and you want only the totals row mapped, use this transformer.
RREEQQUUIIRREEDD EEDDIITTIIOONNSS
USER PERMISSIONS NEEDED
To apply transformers: Admin

22.. Create a data stream, or edit an existing one.
33.. In the Preview Source screen, click Edit Transformers. File transformers are added by default.
44.. In the Transformers in Use section, next to the Total Rows transformer, click Edit.
55.. If you don’t want to upload the total rows from the file to the platform, select the Remove Total Rows
checkbox. This option is selected by default because total rows usually cause the file process to fail.
66.. Click Done.
Transpose Transformers in Marketing Cloud Intelligence
Use a transpose transformer when you want to reconstruct a flat-file format by translating columns of
data into rows. For example, if a file has three conversion types in three separate columns that you want
to aggregate into “total conversions,” they must be transposed into rows from columns. A column of data
in a flat-file corresponds to a field that could be mapped in the Marketing Cloud Intelligence data model.
So anytime you can streamline the number of potential mapped fields, you free up fields that can be
used for other dimensions or measurements. After transpose settings are set for a data stream, new files
uploaded to the data stream automatically inherit the predefined pattern that was specified upon initial
integration.
The transpose process is based on column indexes rather than names. For example, you select a column
named “Click Conversion” to be the first column, and its position in the first file is #10. In the next
uploaded file, the platform starts the transpose on column #10, no matter its name, which is helpful
when your column order changes between files.
If you must transpose dates, such as 01-01-2020 and 02-01-2020, use the Keep Header Value As Is
option when defining the transpose settings. This way, the year, which is a repeating word, isn’t separated
from the rest of the dates.
Note Don’t map non-transposed measurements in the same data stream as the transposed
measurements because their values are multiplied by the number of dimension values under the
transposed object. If you want to map non-transposed measurements, create a second data stream
using the same file. In the first transposed data stream, only include the measurements to be
transposed. Include all the other measurements in the second data stream.
